source.definition: to go or come after someone or something, to move behind or pursue

explanation.sourceText: The English verb "follow" originates from Old English "folgian", meaning to act in accordance with or to come after. It is related to similar words in other Germanic languages.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul englezesc „follow” provine din engleza veche „folgian”, care înseamnă a acționa în conformitate cu ceva sau a veni după ceva. Este înrudit cu cuvinte similare din alte limbi germanice.

source.additional: A strong verb commonly used to describe the action of moving behind or pursuing someone or something.

target.definition: a merge după cineva sau ceva

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian verb „a urma“ derives from the Latin „sequi“, meaning to follow or to go after someone.
explanation.targetText: Verbul românesc „a urma“ derivă din latină, de la „sequi“, însemnând a urma sau a merge după cineva.

target.additional: Regular verb of the 1st conjugation (-a). Frequently used to describe following people or ideas.

explanation.targetText: Este folosit pentru a urma fizic pe cineva.

explanation.sourceText: Also used to follow someone in a conversation.
explanation.targetText: Este folosit și pentru a urmări o discuție.

learningTips.text: Remember "follow" sounds like "fall low" — imagine falling behind someone as you follow them.
Think of "a urma" as similar to "a trail" or "a trace" — you follow a trail to find your way.
The English word "follow" and Romanian "urma" both relate to moving behind or tracking someone.
Picture following footsteps ("urme" in Romanian) to remember "a urma" means to follow.
